enrichment.short_description: Actiona is a free, open-source, cross-platform desktop automation tool that lets users build and run action lists to control the mouse, keyboard, windows, and files, either with a visual drag-and-drop editor or JavaScript scripting.
enrichment.company_size: Solo maintainer with community contributors (open-source project, no company)
enrichment.seo_title: Actiona Review 2026: Free Open-Source Desktop Automation Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Actiona is a free, open-source automation tool for Windows and Linux that scripts mouse clicks, keystrokes, and desktop tasks. Read features, pricing, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Actiona, formerly known as Actionaz, is a free and open-source task automation application for desktop computers, originally created in 2005 by French software engineer Jonathan Mercier-Ganady and still maintained by him with community contributions. The software is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3, with some bundled components under MIT, BSD, and Creative Commons licenses. Actiona is not a commercial product; there is no company behind it, and development is funded through voluntary donations and sponsorships that cover server hosting and code-signing certificate costs. Actiona runs on Windows and Linux and lets users build lists of actions that emulate mouse clicks, mouse movement, keyboard presses, and text entry. Beyond input emulation, it can run system commands, open URLs, display notifications and message boxes, play sounds, search the screen for images or pixel colors, and manipulate application windows. It also supports file operations such as reading and writing text, binary, and INI files, working with the system clipboard and environment variables, copying or downloading files, and sending emails. Flow-control actions let users create loops, jump to labels, pause execution, wait for a specific window or date and time, and stop or exit a running script. Two authoring modes are available: a beginner-friendly visual editor for assembling actions via drag and drop, and a JavaScript (EcmaScript) scripting mode for advanced users who want conditional logic, custom functions, or integration with other tools. Because it requires no programming knowledge to get started, Actiona is popular for repetitive desktop chores like form filling, file organization, scheduled backups, testing UI interactions, and general keyboard-and-mouse macro recording, making it a free alternative to commercial RPA and macro tools such as AutoHotkey or UiPath for individual users. The project remains actively developed, with releases continuing into 2026, source code hosted publicly on GitHub, and a Discord community for support and discussion. Since it is entirely free and open source, Actiona has no pricing tiers, subscriptions, or enterprise licensing; anyone can download, use, modify, and redistribute it under the terms of the GPLv3 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Actiona
paragraphs: Actiona is a free, open-source automation tool that lets anyone script repetitive desktop tasks without writing code, though advanced users can extend it with JavaScript. It began life in 2005 as Actionaz, created by French developer Jonathan Mercier-Ganady, and has since been renamed and continuously maintained as a community-driven project.

The tool works by assembling ordered lists of actions, such as clicking, typing, opening a URL, or waiting for a window, into a single automated script that Actiona then executes. This makes it well suited to simple macro recording as well as more elaborate desktop workflows.

Because Actiona has no company or commercial backing, it is distributed entirely free of charge under the GNU General Public License version 3, with the project funded through community donations rather than subscriptions or licensing fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Actiona can emulate mouse clicks, mouse movement, and keyboard input, and can also detect on-screen images or specific pixel colors to trigger actions, which is useful for automating interactions with applications that lack an API.

It supports system-level actions such as running shell commands, opening files or URLs, showing notifications and message boxes, playing sounds, and controlling application windows, alongside file and data actions like reading or writing text and INI files, using the clipboard, and sending emails.

For more advanced automation, Actiona exposes a full JavaScript (EcmaScript) scripting environment so users can add loops, conditional logic, labels, and custom functions rather than relying solely on the visual editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Actiona is completely free to download, use, and modify, since it is released as open-source software under the GPLv3 license. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or usage limits.

The project accepts voluntary donations and sponsorships to help cover infrastructure costs such as server hosting and code-signing certificates for Windows builds, but these contributions are optional and do not unlock additional features.

features.title: Visual action editor
features.description: Build automation scripts by dragging and dropping actions into an ordered list, with no coding required to get started.

features.title: JavaScript scripting
features.description: Extend or fully replace the visual workflow with EcmaScript code for conditional logic, loops, and custom functions.

features.title: Mouse and keyboard emulation
features.description: Automate clicks, mouse movement, mouse wheel actions, key presses, and text entry across any application.

features.title: Screen and window detection
features.description: Search the screen for images or wait for specific pixel colors, and find, move, resize, or control application windows.

features.title: System and file actions
features.description: Run shell commands, open URLs, read and write text, binary, and INI files, and manipulate the clipboard or environment variables.

features.title: Flow control
features.description: Use loops, labels, pauses, and conditional jumps to build multi-step automation sequences.

features.title: Notifications and messaging
features.description: Trigger message boxes, system notifications, sounds, and outgoing emails as part of an automated script.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on Windows and Linux, with active builds and releases maintained on GitHub.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers or licensing costs
No programming knowledge required for basic automation, thanks to the visual editor
JavaScript scripting available for advanced, highly customized automations
Actively maintained with regular releases and a public GitHub repository
Lightweight desktop tool that does not require a cloud account or subscription
cons: No macOS support, limiting it to Windows and Linux users
Community-maintained project with no dedicated commercial support or SLA
Documentation is spread across a wiki and community channels rather than a polished help center
Less suited to enterprise-scale RPA deployments compared to commercial automation platforms

faqs.question: Is Actiona free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. Actiona is fully free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no paid plans, subscriptions, or feature limits.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Actiona support
faqs.answer: Actiona runs on Windows and Linux. It does not currently offer a macOS build.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use Actiona
faqs.answer: No. Actiona includes a visual drag-and-drop editor for building action lists without coding, though it also supports JavaScript scripting for advanced users.

faqs.question: Who develops Actiona
faqs.answer: Actiona is maintained by French developer Jonathan Mercier-Ganady, who created the project in 2005 under the earlier name Actionaz, along with community contributors.

faqs.question: What was Actiona previously called
faqs.answer: Actiona was originally released as Actionaz before being renamed.

faqs.question: Can Actiona interact with specific applications
faqs.answer: Yes. Actiona can find and control application windows, search the screen for images or colors, and emulate mouse and keyboard input to interact with almost any desktop application.

faqs.question: Is there official support if something breaks
faqs.answer: Support is community-based through the project wiki and Discord server rather than a dedicated commercial support team, since Actiona is a volunteer-maintained open-source project.

use_cases.title: Repetitive desktop task automation

use_cases.audience: Individual users and IT hobbyists

use_cases.title: Custom scripted workflows

use_cases.audience: Power users and open-source contributors

features: Full access to all automation actions
Community support via wiki and Discord

enrichment.short_description: Acumatica is a cloud-based ERP platform for mid-market businesses that unifies financials, distribution, manufacturing, project accounting, and CRM, priced by resource usage rather than per-user seats.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, with entry-level editions typically starting around 6,400 to 7,000 dollars per year
enrichment.seo_title: Acumatica Review 2026: Cloud ERP Pricing, Features and Editions
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Acumatica cloud ERP offers mid-market businesses, including editions, resource-based pricing, key modules, pros and cons, and frequently asked questions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Acumatica is a cloud-based enterprise resource planning (ERP) platform aimed primarily at mid-market and growing businesses. It was founded in 2008 by ERP industry veterans, including John Howell, Serguei Beloussov, and Mike Chtchelkonogov, and is headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, in the Seattle metropolitan area, with additional offices across North America, Europe, Asia, and Australasia. The company employs more than 400 people and serves over 9,000 customers worldwide through a network of value-added resellers and partners who handle sales, implementation, and support. Acumatica's platform covers financial management, distribution and inventory, manufacturing, project accounting, construction management, retail-commerce, and customer relationship management, delivered as a unified suite that customers can adopt incrementally by application. The product is offered in five industry-oriented editions, including General Business, Distribution, Manufacturing, Construction, and Retail-Commerce, plus four licensing tiers, Essentials, Select, Prime, and Enterprise, that determine which modules and capabilities are included. What differentiates Acumatica most from typical ERP vendors is its pricing model: rather than charging per named user or per seat, Acumatica charges based on the applications a customer implements and a resource tier determined by transaction volume, data storage, and computing consumption. This lets an organization add unlimited users at no extra licensing cost, which the company markets heavily as an advantage over legacy per-seat ERP pricing. Typical annual subscription costs range widely, from roughly 6,400 to 7,000 dollars per year for small-business entry tiers, to 25,000 to 75,000 dollars per year for mid-market deployments with 50 to 200 users, up to 150,000 dollars or more per year for large enterprise implementations with heavy transaction volumes. Acumatica can be deployed as SaaS in the cloud, as a privately hosted cloud instance, or on-premise, giving customers flexibility over data residency and infrastructure control. The platform is built on a modern, open architecture with a documented API, which supports third-party integrations and industry-specific extensions built by Acumatica's partner ecosystem. Because exact costs depend heavily on modules, resource tier, and implementation partner, Acumatica does not publish a fixed price list and instead directs prospective customers to industry-specific pricing calculators or a custom quote process managed through its reseller network.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Acumatica
paragraphs: Acumatica is a cloud ERP platform built for mid-market and growing businesses that need to manage financials, operations, and customer relationships in one connected system. Founded in 2008 and headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, the company sells through a global network of partners rather than directly to most customers.

The platform spans core financial management, distribution, manufacturing, project accounting, construction, retail-commerce, and CRM, delivered as a set of integrated applications that organizations can adopt based on their needs.

Acumatica is best known in the ERP market for its unlimited-user pricing model, which charges based on resource consumption and applications used rather than the number of people who log in, a structural difference from most traditional ERP licensing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Acumatica offers five industry editions, General Business, Distribution, Manufacturing, Construction, and Retail-Commerce, each pre-configured with relevant workflows, plus four licensing tiers, Essentials, Select, Prime, and Enterprise, that scale functionality to company size.
Deployment flexibility is a core feature: customers can run Acumatica as public SaaS, private cloud, or on-premise, and can move between deployment types as their infrastructure needs change.

The platform's open, API-first architecture supports a large partner and ISV ecosystem, enabling integrations with e-commerce platforms, payment processors, payroll systems, and industry-specific add-ons.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Acumatica does not use per-user licensing. Instead, cost is driven by which applications a customer implements and by a resource tier based on transaction volume, data storage, and computing usage, allowing unlimited user logins at no added licensing cost.

Published estimates put entry-level General Business Edition subscriptions at roughly 6,400 to 7,000 dollars per year, with mid-market deployments commonly running 25,000 to 75,000 dollars annually and large enterprise implementations reaching well over 150,000 dollars per year depending on modules and scale.

Because pricing depends on edition, resource tier, deployment model, and implementation partner, Acumatica directs prospective buyers to industry-specific pricing calculators or a custom quote from an authorized reseller rather than publishing a fixed price list.

features.title: Unlimited-user pricing
features.description: Resource-based licensing that lets organizations add users without incurring additional per-seat costs.

features.title: CRM and customer management

pros: Unlimited-user pricing avoids per-seat costs as a company grows
Broad, unified functionality spanning financials, distribution, manufacturing, and CRM
Flexible deployment options across SaaS, private cloud, and on-premise
Strong partner and implementation ecosystem for industry-specific customization
Modern, API-first architecture that supports integrations more easily than legacy ERP systems
cons: Pricing is not published, requiring a custom quote through a reseller for accurate costs
Implementation typically requires an experienced partner, adding time and services cost
Resource-based pricing can become expensive as transaction volume and data usage grow
Feature depth and configuration complexity can be significant for smaller organizations

faqs.question: How much does Acumatica cost
faqs.answer: Acumatica does not publish fixed prices. Entry-level editions typically start around 6,400 to 7,000 dollars per year, with mid-market deployments commonly costing 25,000 to 75,000 dollars annually and large enterprise implementations exceeding 150,000 dollars per year, depending on modules and resource tier.

faqs.question: Does Acumatica charge per user
faqs.answer: No. Acumatica uses a resource-based pricing model tied to applications implemented and transaction or data volume, which allows unlimited user logins without additional per-seat fees.

faqs.question: Who founded Acumatica and when
faqs.answer: Acumatica was founded in 2008 by ERP industry veterans including John Howell, Serguei Beloussov, and Mike Chtchelkonogov.

faqs.question: Where is Acumatica headquartered
faqs.answer: Acumatica is headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, in the Seattle metropolitan area, with additional offices internationally.

faqs.question: Can Acumatica be deployed on-premise
faqs.answer: Yes. In addition to public cloud SaaS, Acumatica supports private cloud and on-premise deployment models.

faqs.question: What industries does Acumatica serve
faqs.answer: Acumatica offers dedicated editions for General Business, Distribution, Manufacturing, Construction, and Retail-Commerce, among other supported industries.

faqs.question: Do I need a partner to implement Acumatica
faqs.answer: Most Acumatica customers purchase and implement the platform through an authorized value-added reseller or partner, who handles configuration, customization, and support.

enrichment.short_description: Acunote is a longstanding online project management and Scrum tool from Pluron Inc, offering sprint boards, burndown charts, and wiki-based collaboration for agile software teams, though it is a small, lower-profile product with limited recent public activity.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 5 users; paid plans start at 49 dollars per month
enrichment.company_size: Very small team, reported as approximately 1 employee as of 2026
enrichment.seo_title: Acunote Review 2026: Scrum Project Management Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Acunote is an agile project management and Scrum tool with burndown charts and sprint tracking. See pricing, features, pros, cons, and how active it still is.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Acunote is an online project management and Scrum software product built by Pluron Inc, a small, self-funded, bootstrapped software company headquartered in Foster City, California, in Silicon Valley. Sources give conflicting founding dates, with some indicating the company was incorporated around 2006 and others citing 2011, so an exact founding year cannot be confirmed with confidence. Acunote positions itself as agile project management software usable by organizations of any size, from small teams up to enterprises, and is designed to serve as a company-wide tool for tracking software development, IT projects, and general team collaboration rather than only engineering-specific work. Its core functionality centers on Scrum: teams create projects and sprints, assign and track tasks from a central task list screen, and monitor progress through burndown charts that are generated from actual logged work rather than manual updates, aiming to give more accurate forecasts of sprint and project completion. Each project and task includes an integrated wiki with a rich text editor for documentation and collaboration. Acunote supports role-based access control and offers different levels of analytics and reporting depending on plan tier, ranging from basic sprint-level analytics to enterprise-wide reporting across multiple departments. Historically, Acunote emphasized integrations with widely used developer tools of its era, including source control systems such as Subversion, GitHub, Bitbucket, and Perforce, and bug trackers such as JIRA, Trac, FogBugz, Bugzilla, and Mantis, as well as a listing in the Google Workspace Marketplace as a Scrum project management add-on for G Suite. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free plan supports individuals or small teams of up to 5 users, while paid plans, Business at 49 dollars per month, Corporate at 99 dollars per month, and Enterprise at 149 dollars per month, each include 7 users with additional users billed incrementally, plus a 30-day free trial and no long-term contract requirement. Independent company-data sources report that Acunote and Pluron Inc currently operate with a very small team, cited as roughly a single employee as of 2026, which is a notable decline from prior years and suggests the product is maintained in a low-overhead, largely legacy mode rather than under active growth or heavy new feature development. The website remains online and accessible, with login and marketing pages functioning, but the presence of dated integrations like Subversion and Trac, alongside minimal visible recent updates, indicates limited current development activity. Prospective buyers should treat Acunote as a stable but low-profile legacy tool rather than an actively evolving modern SaaS product, and should verify current support responsiveness directly before committing a team to it.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Acunote
paragraphs: Acunote is an online project management and Scrum tool built by Pluron Inc, a small, bootstrapped software company based in Foster City, California. It is designed to support agile software teams as well as broader company-wide project tracking.

The product centers on Scrum workflows, including sprint planning, a central task list, burndown charts driven by actual execution data, and an integrated project and task wiki for documentation and collaboration.

Acunote has been on the market for many years and integrates with legacy developer tools such as Subversion, JIRA, Trac, and Bugzilla, reflecting its origins earlier in the history of agile software tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform provides sprint and project management from a single task list view, burndown charts based on logged execution rather than manual status updates, and role-based access control for teams of varying sizes.
Analytics and reporting scale with plan tier, from basic sprint-level views on the entry paid plan to advanced, multi-team, and enterprise-wide reporting on higher tiers.
Integrated wikis attached to projects and tasks give teams a place for documentation, and the product connects with source control systems and bug trackers commonly used by software teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Acunote offers a free plan for individuals or teams of up to 5 users, alongside three paid plans: Business at 49 dollars per month, Corporate at 99 dollars per month, and Enterprise at 149 dollars per month, each including 7 users with additional users billed per seat.
All paid plans include a 30-day free trial with no credit card required upfront, and customers can upgrade, downgrade, or cancel without long-term contracts.

Because the company appears to operate with a very small team as of 2026, buyers evaluating Acunote should confirm current support levels and product roadmap directly before adopting it for a growing team.

features.title: Developer tool integrations

Integrations lean on legacy tools like Subversion and Trac rather than many modern developer platforms

Less modern interface and feature depth than newer agile project management tools

faqs.question: Is Acunote still active in 2026
faqs.answer: The website and login remain online and functional, and the company reports paid customers, but Acunote appears to operate with a very small team, so recent development activity is limited. Buyers should verify current support directly before adopting it.

faqs.question: How much does Acunote cost
faqs.answer: Acunote has a free plan for up to 5 users, and paid plans starting at 49 dollars per month for the Business tier, up to 149 dollars per month for the Enterprise tier, each including 7 users.

faqs.question: Who makes Acunote
faqs.answer: Acunote is made by Pluron Inc, a small, self-funded software company based in Foster City, California.

faqs.question: Does Acunote integrate with GitHub and JIRA
faqs.answer: Yes. Acunote integrates with source control systems including GitHub, Bitbucket, Subversion, and Perforce, and bug trackers including JIRA, Trac, FogBugz, Bugzilla, and Mantis.

faqs.question: What is Acunote used for
faqs.answer: Acunote is used for agile Scrum project management, including sprint planning, task tracking, burndown charting, and team collaboration through an integrated wiki.

faqs.question: Does Acunote offer a free trial
faqs.answer: Yes. All paid plans include a 30-day free trial with no credit card required, in addition to a permanently free plan for small teams.

faqs.question: Is there a Google Workspace version of Acunote
faqs.answer: Yes. Acunote is listed in the Google Workspace Marketplace as a Scrum project management tool for G Suite.

enrichment.short_description: Ada is an enterprise AI customer service platform that deploys AI agents across chat, voice, email, and messaging channels to autonomously resolve high volumes of customer support conversations.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, contact sales; enterprise contracts commonly start around 30,000 dollars per year
enrichment.seo_title: Ada (ada.cx) Review 2026: AI Customer Service Platform Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ada is an enterprise AI customer service platform for automating support conversations at scale. See its features, pricing model, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ada is an enterprise AI customer service platform, headquartered in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, and founded in 2016 by Mike Murchison and David Hariri. The company reached unicorn status in 2021 after a 130 million dollar Series C funding round led by Spark Capital that valued the business at 1.2 billion dollars, and it currently employs roughly 300 to 500 people. Ada's platform, referred to as the ACX or Agentic Customer Experience Platform, is built around four core components. The Reasoning Engine is a unified intelligence layer that powers consistent AI agent responses using shared customer context and multiple safeguard layers, and its latest version launched in February 2026. The Conversation Hub deploys AI agents across a wide range of channels, including voice, email, chat, SMS, Instagram, in-app messaging, and custom channels, enabling omnichannel customer experiences from a single underlying agent. The Performance Center gives teams visibility and control through tools such as Playbooks for defining multi-step workflows, Coaching for continuous improvement of agent behavior, and Simulations for safely testing changes before they go live. The Developer Toolkit provides APIs, SDKs, and an MCP server so engineering teams can integrate Ada's AI agents with existing enterprise systems and data sources. Ada targets large, often regulated, high-volume industries including ecommerce, financial services, gaming, health insurance, property and casualty insurance, SaaS, and travel, and it emphasizes compliance certifications such as HIPAA, SOC 2, and GDPR to meet enterprise security requirements. Its customer base includes well-known brands such as Pinterest, Monday.com, Cebu Pacific, and Ipsy, operating across more than 85 countries. Ada uses conversation-based pricing, meaning customers pay based on the volume of conversations their AI agents handle rather than per-seat licensing, which the company positions as predictable and scalable. Pricing is not publicly published; Ada's own materials indicate the platform is designed for companies with at least 300,000 annual customer service conversations, placing it firmly in enterprise territory rather than serving small businesses or startups. Third-party estimates suggest that entry-level annual contracts commonly start around 30,000 dollars, while larger enterprise deployments can run from 100,000 to 300,000 dollars or more per year depending on conversation volume and configuration. Prospective customers must contact Ada's sales team directly for a custom quote.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Ada
paragraphs: Ada is an enterprise AI customer service platform founded in 2016 and headquartered in Toronto, Canada. It builds AI agents that autonomously handle customer support conversations across channels such as chat, voice, email, and messaging apps.

The company's platform is organized around its Reasoning Engine, a unified intelligence layer that keeps AI agent responses consistent regardless of which channel a customer uses, backed by shared context and multiple safety layers.

Ada is built specifically for large-scale, often regulated industries such as financial services, insurance, and travel, and its own materials indicate the platform is intended for organizations handling at least 300,000 customer service conversations per year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Conversation Hub deploys AI agents across voice, email, chat, SMS, Instagram, in-app messaging, and custom channels, allowing a single underlying agent to serve customers wherever they reach out.
The Performance Center provides Playbooks for defining multi-step workflows, Coaching tools for continuously refining agent behavior, and Simulations for testing changes safely before deployment.

A Developer Toolkit with APIs, SDKs, and an MCP server lets engineering teams connect Ada's agents to existing enterprise systems, while compliance certifications such as HIPAA, SOC 2, and GDPR support regulated industries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ada uses conversation-based pricing, charging based on the volume of conversations its AI agents handle rather than a per-agent seat fee, which is intended to scale predictably with usage.

Pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting Ada's sales team for a custom quote. Ada's own pricing page indicates the platform is designed for companies with at least 300,000 annual customer service conversations.

Third-party estimates put typical entry-level annual contracts at around 30,000 dollars, with larger enterprise agreements commonly ranging from 100,000 to 300,000 dollars or more per year depending on scale and configuration.

Implementation and configuration complexity is likely higher than lightweight chatbot tools

faqs.question: What is Ada used for
faqs.answer: Ada is used to deploy AI agents that autonomously handle customer service conversations across chat, voice, email, and messaging channels for large enterprises.

faqs.question: How much does Ada cost
faqs.answer: Ada does not publish fixed pricing. It uses conversation-based pricing with custom quotes, and entry-level annual contracts commonly start around 30,000 dollars, while large enterprise deployments can reach 100,000 to 300,000 dollars or more per year.

faqs.question: Who founded Ada and when
faqs.answer: Ada was founded in 2016 by Mike Murchison and David Hariri and is headquartered in Toronto, Canada.

faqs.question: Is Ada suitable for small businesses
faqs.answer: Not typically. Ada's platform is designed for organizations with at least 300,000 annual customer service conversations, positioning it as an enterprise-focused product.

faqs.question: What channels does Ada support
faqs.answer: Ada supports voice, email, chat, SMS, Instagram, in-app messaging, and custom channels through its Conversation Hub.

faqs.question: Does Ada offer developer tools
faqs.answer: Yes. Ada provides a Developer Toolkit with APIs, SDKs, and an MCP server for integrating with existing enterprise systems.

faqs.question: Is Ada compliant with industry regulations
faqs.answer: Yes. Ada supports HIPAA, SOC 2, and GDPR compliance, targeting regulated industries such as health insurance, financial services, and property and casualty insurance.

enrichment.short_description: Adaptable is a deployment platform that connects to a GitHub repository and automatically builds, deploys, and manages the infrastructure for full-stack applications, including databases, load balancing, and SSL.
enrichment.starting_price: From 3 dollars per month plus resource usage
enrichment.seo_title: Adaptable.io Review 2026: GitHub-Connected App Deployment Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Adaptable.io deploys full-stack apps straight from a GitHub repo, handling databases, load balancing, and SSL automatically. See pricing, features, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Adaptable, also known as Adaptable.io, is a deployment automation platform founded in 2022 and headquartered in Denver, Colorado. The company describes its product as the easiest way to deploy an application: a developer connects a GitHub repository and Adaptable handles the rest, from building the app to provisioning the infrastructure it needs to run in production. Rather than requiring users to manually configure servers, databases, load balancers, content delivery networks, and SSL and TLS certificates, Adaptable automates that provisioning based on templates tied to the type of application being deployed. Each time a developer pushes new code to their selected branch on GitHub, Adaptable automatically rebuilds the application and updates the live deployment, functioning as a continuous deployment pipeline built directly on top of Git workflows. The platform also supports preview deployments, letting teams designate a development branch that gets its own deployment for testing changes before merging to production. Adaptable is designed to host a wide range of web-based applications, including front-end interfaces, back-end services, and the supporting infrastructure they depend on, positioning it as an alternative to manually wiring together separate hosting, database, and networking providers. App templates handle the specifics of building an application and defining the cloud resources it needs, including serverless containers and managed databases, reducing the DevOps configuration typically required to get a full-stack app into production. Adaptable's pricing combines fixed and usage-based plans. A fixed-price plan starts at 3 dollars per month with a 10-day free trial and no usage charges, subject to resource usage limits per app. A usage-based plan for individual apps starts at 5 dollars per month per app plus resource usage, which includes a 5 dollar resource usage credit. A usage-based organization plan starts at 20 dollars per month per organization plus resource usage, also including a 5 dollar credit and support for up to 3 users. This tiered structure lets solo developers and small teams start cheaply while scaling costs with actual infrastructure consumption as an application grows. Because Adaptable is a smaller, more recently founded company, detailed public information about its employee count, funding, and broader company history is limited compared to larger, more established infrastructure providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Adaptable
paragraphs: Adaptable, known online as Adaptable.io, is a deployment platform founded in 2022 and based in Denver, Colorado. It is built around a simple premise: connect a GitHub repository, and Adaptable automatically builds and deploys the application along with the infrastructure it needs.

Rather than requiring developers to manually configure servers, databases, load balancers, CDNs, and SSL certificates, Adaptable provisions this infrastructure automatically using templates matched to the type of application being deployed.

The platform functions as a continuous deployment pipeline: every push to a selected GitHub branch triggers a new build and updates the live deployment, and teams can configure preview deployments for development branches before merging changes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Adaptable connects directly to GitHub repositories and rebuilds an application automatically on every push, removing manual deployment steps from a team's workflow.
App templates define the cloud resources an application needs, such as serverless containers and managed databases, so developers do not have to configure infrastructure by hand.
The platform can host full-stack applications, including front-end, back-end, and supporting services, and it provisions SSL and TLS, load balancing, and CDN automatically as part of deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Adaptable offers a fixed-price plan starting at 3 dollars per month with a 10-day free trial and no usage-based charges, subject to per-app resource limits.

A usage-based plan for individual apps starts at 5 dollars per month per app plus resource usage, including a 5 dollar usage credit, while a usage-based organization plan starts at 20 dollars per month per organization plus resource usage, including a 5 dollar credit and support for up to 3 users.
This combination of fixed and usage-based tiers lets individual developers start with predictable low costs while teams and growing applications scale spend with actual infrastructure consumption.

faqs.answer: Adaptable automatically provisions resources such as serverless containers, databases, load balancers, CDN, and SSL and TLS certificates based on app templates.

faqs.answer: Adaptable offers a fixed-price plan from 3 dollars per month, a usage-based per-app plan from 5 dollars per month plus resource usage, and a usage-based organization plan from 20 dollars per month plus resource usage.

faqs.answer: Yes. Teams can select a development branch to receive its own preview deployment for testing before merging changes.

faqs.question: Where is Adaptable headquartered
faqs.answer: Adaptable is headquartered in Denver, Colorado, United States, and was founded in 2022.

faqs.answer: Adaptable is designed to host most web-based applications, including front-end interfaces, back-end services, and the supporting infrastructure they need.

enrichment.short_description: Adapty is a mobile app subscription platform that helps developers manage in-app purchases, run A/B tests on paywalls, and analyze subscription revenue metrics across iOS and Android without writing extra client code.
enrichment.starting_price: Free until 5,000 dollars in monthly revenue, then 1 percent of monthly revenue
enrichment.seo_title: Adapty.io Review 2026: Mobile Subscription Analytics and Paywall Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Adapty.io helps mobile apps manage subscriptions, A/B test paywalls, and track revenue analytics. See pricing, key features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Adapty is a subscription management and monetization platform for mobile applications, founded in 2019 by two developers and headquartered in New York City. The company operates as a globally remote team, reportedly grown to over 150 employees across multiple continents as of 2026, and states that its infrastructure is trusted by more than 20,000 apps. Adapty's core purpose is to help mobile developers manage in-app purchases and subscriptions across iOS and Android without having to build and maintain their own backend billing and analytics infrastructure. The platform provides open-source SDKs for all major mobile platforms, so developers can integrate subscription handling with minimal native code, along with support for web payments through providers such as Stripe, Paddle, or custom payment integrations. Adapty synchronizes subscription state across platforms, giving apps a unified view of a user's subscription status regardless of where they purchased it. On the analytics side, Adapty tracks core subscription revenue metrics including monthly recurring revenue, churn, and average revenue per user and lifetime value, letting product and growth teams understand monetization performance in real time. A significant part of the platform is its no-code paywall and onboarding builder, which ships with more than 50 templates and supports A/B testing and user segmentation so teams can experiment with pricing, design, and messaging without shipping new app binaries for every test. Adapty has also added AI-powered capabilities, including predictive analytics and an AI paywall generator, to help teams optimize conversion faster. Adapty's pricing follows a freemium, revenue-share model rather than flat subscription tiers. On its Pro plan, revenue is tracked on a rolling 30-day basis with no charge until an app crosses 5,000 dollars in monthly revenue; once that threshold is crossed, Adapty begins charging 1 percent of that month's revenue. An Enterprise plan is available with custom pricing for apps operating at larger scale or with more complex business models, adding white-glove onboarding and migration assistance, a dedicated Slack channel with 24/7 support, a dedicated customer success manager, custom data exports and contracts, and data residency options in the United States or European Union, along with SOC 2 certification and GDPR compliance. Adapty also offers several optional add-ons billed separately, including Refund Saver, UA Tools for attributed install tracking, Adapty Mail, and an Apple Ads Manager, each with its own free tier and usage-based pricing beyond a revenue threshold.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Adapty.io
paragraphs: Adapty is a mobile app subscription management and monetization platform, founded in 2019 and headquartered in New York City. It helps developers manage in-app purchases and analyze subscription revenue without building custom backend billing infrastructure.
The company operates as a globally remote team that has grown to more than 150 employees and states its infrastructure powers over 20,000 apps, spanning iOS and Android.

Adapty's product combines subscription management SDKs, cross-platform synchronization, revenue analytics, and a no-code paywall builder into a single platform aimed at mobile growth and product teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Open-source SDKs for all major mobile platforms let developers integrate subscription handling with minimal native code, while support for web payments through Stripe, Paddle, or custom integrations extends monetization beyond app stores.
Real-time revenue analytics cover metrics such as monthly recurring revenue, churn, average revenue per user, and lifetime value, giving teams visibility into subscription performance.

A no-code paywall and onboarding builder with more than 50 templates supports A/B testing and user segmentation, and AI-powered tools including predictive analytics and an AI paywall generator help teams optimize conversion faster.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Adapty's Pro plan is free until an app crosses 5,000 dollars in monthly revenue, tracked on a rolling 30-day basis; once that threshold is crossed, Adapty charges 1 percent of that month's revenue.

An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for larger apps, adding white-glove onboarding, a dedicated Slack channel with 24/7 support, a dedicated success manager, custom data exports and contracts, and choice of US or EU data residency, along with SOC 2 and GDPR compliance.

Optional add-ons, including Refund Saver, UA Tools, Adapty Mail, and Apple Ads Manager, are billed separately and generally follow the same free-until-threshold, then usage-based or revenue-share pricing pattern.

No-code paywall builder and A/B testing let teams iterate without shipping new app versions
Broad SDK and payment integration support across iOS, Android, and web payments
Real-time subscription analytics purpose-built for mobile monetization
Enterprise-grade compliance options including SOC 2 and GDPR for larger customers
cons: Revenue-share pricing model means costs scale directly with app revenue rather than a flat fee
Several advanced capabilities are gated behind separate paid add-ons

Best suited to apps with in-app purchase or subscription models rather than other monetization types

faqs.question: How much does Adapty cost
faqs.answer: Adapty's Pro plan is free until an app reaches 5,000 dollars in monthly revenue, after which it charges 1 percent of that month's revenue. An Enterprise plan with custom pricing is available for larger apps.

faqs.question: What platforms does Adapty support
faqs.answer: Adapty provides SDKs for iOS and Android, plus support for web payments through providers like Stripe and Paddle.

faqs.question: Can I A/B test paywalls with Adapty
faqs.answer: Yes. Adapty includes a no-code paywall and onboarding builder with more than 50 templates that supports A/B testing and user segmentation.

faqs.question: When was Adapty founded
faqs.answer: Adapty was founded in 2019 and is headquartered in New York City.

faqs.question: Does Adapty offer analytics for subscription revenue
faqs.answer: Yes. Adapty tracks metrics such as monthly recurring revenue, churn, average revenue per user, and lifetime value.

faqs.question: Is Adapty suitable for enterprise apps
faqs.answer: Yes. Adapty offers an Enterprise plan with custom pricing, dedicated support, data residency options, and SOC 2 and GDPR compliance.

faqs.question: Are there additional costs beyond the core Adapty plan
faqs.answer: Yes. Adapty offers optional add-ons such as Refund Saver, UA Tools, Adapty Mail, and Apple Ads Manager, each billed separately with its own pricing.

enrichment.short_description: AdCreative.ai is an AI-powered ad creative platform that generates conversion-focused static and video ads and social media posts for Meta, Google, and LinkedIn, now owned by marketing technology company Appier.
enrichment.company_size: 60-plus employees at the time of its 2025 acquisition
enrichment.headquarters: Paris, France, with an R and D team based in Turkey
enrichment.seo_title: AdCreative.ai Review 2026: AI Ad Generator Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdCreative.ai uses AI to generate high-performing ad creatives for Meta, Google, and LinkedIn. See pricing plans, key features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AdCreative.ai is an AI-powered advertising creative generation platform founded in 2021 and headquartered in Paris, France, with its research and development team based in Turkey. The company grew quickly, reaching more than 60 employees and an annual recurring revenue of roughly 15.9 million dollars within about 18 months of launch. On February 12, 2025, AdCreative.ai was acquired by Appier Technologies, a publicly listed marketing technology company, in a deal reported at approximately 38.7 million dollars, as part of Appier's strategy to expand its generative AI advertising and marketing capabilities. The platform allows marketers and small businesses to instantly generate high-performing static and video ad creatives, along with organic social media posts, tailored for placement on Meta, Google, LinkedIn, and other advertising channels. Core functionality includes AI-generated ad creative variations, AI image generation, brand customization using a business's logos and colors, and a large library of templates for both paid ads and organic social content. A creative scoring feature evaluates generated ads before they go live, aiming to predict which variations are most likely to perform well, and the platform supports generating multiple design variations and ad sizes from a single input. AdCreative.ai integrates with major ad platforms and tools, including Google Ads, Meta, and Google Drive, to streamline the workflow from creative generation to campaign deployment, and it uses natural language processing so users can describe what they want in plain text rather than manually designing each asset. Reviews indicate the tool's primary user base, roughly 95 percent, consists of small businesses and marketers looking for fast creative production, though some reviewers note mixed satisfaction with creative quality and customer service responsiveness. AdCreative.ai offers three subscription tiers, all billed monthly with no permanent free plan, though a free trial is available: Starter at 39 dollars per month, Professional at 249 dollars per month, which includes 50 monthly credits, 10 brands, and 10 user seats, and Ultimate at 599 dollars per month, which includes 100 monthly credits and 25 brands. Annual billing offers a discount of roughly 40 percent, equivalent to almost two months free, compared to paying monthly.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AdCreative.ai
paragraphs: AdCreative.ai is an AI-powered platform that generates advertising creatives and social media posts, founded in 2021 and headquartered in Paris, France, with its R&D team based in Turkey.

The company grew rapidly to over 60 employees and roughly 15.9 million dollars in annual recurring revenue within about 18 months, and was acquired by marketing technology company Appier Technologies in February 2025 for approximately 38.7 million dollars.
The platform is aimed primarily at marketers and small businesses that need to produce large volumes of ad creative quickly for channels such as Meta, Google, and LinkedIn.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AdCreative.ai generates static and video ad creatives as well as organic social posts, using AI image generation, brand-specific customization with a business's logos and colors, and a large template library.
A creative scoring feature evaluates generated ads to help predict performance before they are launched, and the platform can produce multiple design variations and ad sizes from a single request.
The tool integrates with Google Ads, Meta, and Google Drive, and uses natural language processing so users can describe the creative they want rather than manually designing each asset.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AdCreative.ai offers three monthly subscription tiers: Starter at 39 dollars per month, Professional at 249 dollars per month with 50 monthly credits, 10 brands, and 10 user seats, and Ultimate at 599 dollars per month with 100 monthly credits and 25 brands.
There is no permanent free plan, but a free trial is available for prospective customers to test the platform before subscribing.
Annual billing provides a discount of approximately 40 percent compared to paying month to month, equivalent to nearly two free months per year.

enrichment.short_description: Addepar is a cloud-based wealth management platform that aggregates and analyzes portfolio data for registered investment advisors and financial institutions, powering reporting on trillions of dollars in assets.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, contact sales; fees are typically tiered based on assets under management and user licenses
enrichment.seo_title: Addepar Review 2026: Wealth Management Software Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Addepar is a data aggregation and portfolio reporting platform for registered investment advisors and wealth managers. See its features, pricing, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Addepar is a cloud-based wealth management software platform founded in 2009 by Joseph Lonsdale, Jason Mirra, and Brittney Mickel, and headquartered in Mountain View, California, with additional offices in New York, London, Pune in India, and Dubai. The company has grown substantially, reporting approximately 1,400 employees as of 2026, up from around 1,000 in 2024, reflecting continued expansion of its team. Addepar specializes in data aggregation, analytics, and portfolio reporting for registered investment advisors, family offices, private banks, and other financial professionals who need a unified view of complex, multi-custodian investment portfolios. The platform pulls together data from disparate custodians, asset classes, and account structures into a single system of record, enabling advisors to analyze performance, exposure, and risk across public securities as well as harder-to-value alternative investments such as private equity, real estate, and hedge funds. Addepar states that its platform is trusted by financial professionals globally and powers reporting on more than 9 trillion dollars in assets across more than 1,400 clients in over 60 countries, positioning it as one of the most widely used data infrastructure platforms in the wealth management industry. Revenue is primarily subscription-based, delivered as software as a service, with tiered pricing that generally scales with the assets under management an advisor or firm manages on the platform, plus the number of user licenses required, supplemented by professional services fees for onboarding, data migration, and customization. Addepar does not publish a public price list, and prospective customers must contact the company directly for a quote tailored to their asset base and use case. Some users have expressed frustration with a pricing structure in which fees rise as assets under management grow but do not automatically decrease proportionally if a client's assets under management later falls, a common point of criticism in reviews of the platform. Addepar has continued to expand its capabilities over time, including enhanced reporting, mobile access, third-party integrations, and analytics aimed at both financial advisors and their end clients. As a company handling highly sensitive financial data at scale, Addepar emphasizes security, data integrity, and analytics designed specifically for the operational realities of registered investment advisors and other regulated wealth management firms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Addepar
paragraphs: Addepar is a cloud-based wealth management platform founded in 2009 and headquartered in Mountain View, California, with additional offices in New York, London, Pune, and Dubai. It specializes in aggregating and analyzing investment portfolio data for financial professionals.

The platform serves registered investment advisors, family offices, private banks, and other wealth management firms that need a unified view of client portfolios spanning multiple custodians and both public and alternative asset classes.
Addepar has grown to roughly 1,400 employees and reports that its platform powers reporting on more than 9 trillion dollars in assets across over 1,400 clients in more than 60 countries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Addepar aggregates data from disparate custodians and account structures into a single system of record, giving advisors a consolidated view of complex, multi-account client portfolios.
The platform supports analytics and reporting across both public securities and harder-to-value alternative investments such as private equity, real estate, and hedge funds.
Addepar provides tools for performance measurement, exposure and risk analysis, client-facing reporting, and integrations with other systems used by wealth management firms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Addepar's revenue model is subscription-based, with pricing typically tiered according to the total assets under management on the platform and the number of user licenses a firm requires.
Additional professional services fees generally apply for onboarding, data migration, and customization work when a firm implements the platform.
Addepar does not publish public pricing, so firms must contact the company directly to receive a custom quote based on their asset base and specific needs.

features.description: Analyze harder-to-value investments such as private equity, real estate, and hedge funds alongside public securities.

features.description: Produce polished reports and dashboards that advisors can share directly with their end clients.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connect with custodians, CRM systems, and other wealth management tools used by advisory firms.

features.title: Global reach
features.description: Serve financial professionals and clients across more than 60 countries with international offices supporting operations.

faqs.question: What is Addepar used for
faqs.answer: Addepar is used by registered investment advisors, family offices, and private banks to aggregate, analyze, and report on complex, multi-custodian investment portfolios spanning public and alternative assets.

faqs.question: How much does Addepar cost
faqs.answer: Addepar does not publish fixed pricing. Fees are typically tiered based on assets under management and user licenses, with additional professional services costs, and require contacting the company directly for a quote.

faqs.question: Who founded Addepar and when
faqs.answer: Addepar was founded in 2009 by Joseph Lonsdale, Jason Mirra, and Brittney Mickel.

faqs.question: Where is Addepar headquartered
faqs.answer: Addepar is headquartered in Mountain View, California, with additional offices in New York, London, Pune, India, and Dubai.

faqs.question: How much in assets does Addepar's platform support
faqs.answer: Addepar reports that its platform powers reporting on more than 9 trillion dollars in assets across more than 1,400 clients in over 60 countries.

faqs.question: Does Addepar support alternative investments
faqs.answer: Yes. Addepar provides analytics for harder-to-value alternative investments such as private equity, real estate, and hedge funds, alongside public securities.

faqs.question: Is Addepar suitable for individual investors
faqs.answer: No. Addepar is built for wealth management professionals such as registered investment advisors and family offices, not for direct use by individual retail investors.

use_cases.title: Multi-custodian portfolio reporting
use_cases.description: Consolidate client investment data from multiple custodians into a single system for performance and risk analysis.
use_cases.audience: Registered investment advisors and wealth management firms

use_cases.title: Alternative investment analytics
use_cases.description: Track and analyze private equity, real estate, and hedge fund holdings alongside traditional public market assets.
use_cases.audience: Family offices and institutional wealth managers

use_cases.title: Client reporting and communication
use_cases.description: Generate polished, data-driven reports that advisors can share directly with high-net-worth clients.
use_cases.audience: Financial advisors serving high-net-worth individuals

features: Pricing tiered by assets under management
Professional services for onboarding and customization
